coredata.dc:title: Construction of 3D carbon dots/Prussian blue analogue-derived phosphide nanocomposites on Ni foam for high-performance supercapacitors
coredata.dc:creator: Jia, Pengyun
Yao, Hongchang
Liu, Qingchao
Wang, Jianshe
Li, Zhongjun
Zhang, Lin
coredata.dc:description: A desirable electrode material with rational architecture and superior composition is the key to optimize the electrochemical performance of supercapacitor. Prussian blue analogues are a kind of promising precursors for electrode preparation with the features of adjustable compositions and rigid frame structure. In this work, carbon dots/nickel iron phosphide nanoparticles grown on Ni foam have been delicately designed and synthesized from PBAs for the application of cathode of hybrid supercapacitors (HSC). Introduction of CDs is found that not only favors the nanocubes growth of PBAs precursor but also alleviates aggregation of the nanocubes during calcination and phosphorization. The uniform coverage of carbon dots on the phosphides offers hydrophilic interfaces beneficial to the interaction between electrodes and electrolyte and improves the chemical stability of the electrodes. With these assets, the optimum electrode exhibits remarkable areal capacitance (2589 mF cm−2 at a current density of 2 mA cm−2), noteworthy rate performance and excellent cycling stability. Moreover, the as-fabricated hybrid supercapacitor shows a high energy density and power density, as well as high stability. These results indicate that the CDs/NiFeP-NF electrode has the desirable potential to be a candidate for energy storage devices. The structure modulation in this work may provide a new avenue for development of PBAs-based phosphides materials in the field of energy conversion and storage.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Transitional metal phosphide
Carbon dots
Hybrid supercapacitor
Prussian blue analogues

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of the construction of CDs/NiFeP-NF.
XRD patterns of PBA, CPBA, CP-250, CP-300, P-350,CP-350, CP-400 and CP-500.
SEM images of (a) CP-250, (b) CP-300, (c) CP-350 and (d) P-300.
(a) The elemental mappings, (b-d) TEM images and (e) HRTEM image of CP-350.
(a) Full XPS survey spectrum of CP-350 and High-resolution XPS spectra of (b) Ni 2p, (c) Fe 2p, (d) P 2p, (e) C 1s and (f) N 1s.
FT-IR spectra of PBA, CPBA, CP-250, CP-300, CP-350, CP-400 and CP-500.

(a) CV curves at 5 mV s−1, (b) the plots of log (v) versus log (i) of the PBA、 CPBA、P-350 and CP-350 electrodes, (c) GCD curves at 2 mA cm−2, (d) Specific capacitance at 2–20 mA cm−2, (e) Nyquist plots of EIS and equivalent circuit mode, (f) Cycling performance of PBA、CPBA、P-350 and CP-350 at a current density of 20 mA cm−2 for 3000 cycles.

(a) Schematic illustration of the fabricated HSC, (b) CV curves of CP-350 and AC electrode tested at a scan rate of 5 mV s−1, (c) CV curves in different potential windows at 20 mV s−1, (d) CV curves of the CP-350//AC devices at different scan rates from 5 to 50 mV·s−1 in a voltage window of 0–1.7 V, (e) the charge and discharge curves at different current densities from 5 to 50 mA cm−2, (f) Areal specific capacitance of the CP-350//AC devices at various current densities, (g) Ragone plot of the HSC, (h) Cycle performance of the CP-350//AC devices at a current density of 50 mA cm−2 for 3000 cycles (Inset a bright LED indicator and an mini motor fan powered by the fully charged devices.).

head.ce:title: Construction of 3D carbon dots/Prussian blue analogue-derived phosphide nanocomposites on Ni foam for high-performance supercapacitors

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou 450001, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry, Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou 450001, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A desirable electrode material with rational architecture and superior composition is the key to optimize the electrochemical performance of supercapacitor. Prussian blue analogues are a kind of promising precursors for electrode preparation with the features of adjustable compositions and rigid frame structure. In this work, carbon dots/nickel iron phosphide nanoparticles grown on Ni foam have been delicately designed and synthesized from PBAs for the application of cathode of hybrid supercapacitors (HSC). Introduction of CDs is found that not only favors the nanocubes growth of PBAs precursor but also alleviates aggregation of the nanocubes during calcination and phosphorization. The uniform coverage of carbon dots on the phosphides offers hydrophilic interfaces beneficial to the interaction between electrodes and electrolyte and improves the chemical stability of the electrodes. With these assets, the optimum electrode exhibits remarkable areal capacitance (2589 mF cm−2 at a current density of 2 mA cm−2), noteworthy rate performance and excellent cycling stability. Moreover, the as-fabricated hybrid supercapacitor shows a high energy density and power density, as well as high stability. These results indicate that the CDs/NiFeP-NF electrode has the desirable potential to be a candidate for energy storage devices. The structure modulation in this work may provide a new avenue for development of PBAs-based phosphides materials in the field of energy conversion and storage.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors are unable or have chosen not to specify which data has been used.

ce:section.ce:para: Nowadays, the development of advanced energy conversion and storage systems have been spurred by the stress of energy crisis and climate issues [1]. The hybrid supercapacitors that usually consist of a battery-type cathode and capacitor-type anode are emerging and regarded as a kind of promising energy storage devices due to the high specific energy, long life expectancy and rapid charge/discharge rate [2–4]. The cathode materials are the crucial factor to improve the electrochemical performance. For an ideal cathode, some criteria should be fulfilled including porous structure, high redox activity, high electrical conductivity, chemical stability and etc. [5–7]. However, to design and fabricate a desirable electrode material that meets the requirements is still a full of challenging task.

Recently, Prussian blue analogues (PBAs) have gained much attention in the supercapacitors application, which have the chemical formular of AxM′a[M’’b(CN)6] where M′/M” represent transition metal cations [8]. Because of high compositional flexibility, nontoxic and inexpensive, PBAs have been applied as an ideal precursor to fabricate multi-metal compounds such as oxides, sulfides, phosphides and selenides [9–12]. Especially, metal phosphides have exhibited better electrical conductivity and superior electrochemical stability than those of PBAs precursor, which make them a potential generation of electrode material [13]. However, the structural destruction during calcination of precursors will severely degrade the capacitive performance [14,15]. In addition, the volume expansion of metal phosphides during charge/discharge cycles would result in poor cycling endurances [16,17]. To solve these problems, carbon coating will be an effective method. For an example, a glucose-derived carbon shell was formed to protect Ni2P @ C from oxidation and volumetric expansion of Ni2P upon cycling and the resulted Ni2P @ C electrodes achieve high capacity and excellent cyclability [18].

In nano-carbon family, carbon nanodots (CDs) have an outstanding advantage to construct nanocomposites attributing to their abundant surface functional groups, extraordinary physicochemical properties, easy to synthesis, and low prices [19–21]. Various nanocomposites like CDs/NiFe PBAs [22], CDs/CoFe PBAs [23], CDs/NiCo-Se [24], CDs/Metal-organic framework (CDs/MOF) [25], CDs@PMo12/Ni-MOF [26] and CDs/NiCo2O4 [27] have been reported to show excellent interfaces interaction between electrode and electrolyte and thus improving the capacitive properties and the chemical stability. Therefore, we infer that CDs have the potential to improve the cyclic stability and capacitive performance of metal phosphides through structural optimization and composition regulation.

Herein, a binder-free electrode of CDs/nickel iron phosphides grown on Ni foam (CDs/NiFeP-NF) was designed and synthesized by the simultaneous calcination and phosphorization of NiFe PBAs nanocubes. In the reaction to form PBAs, NF serves as not only conductive substrate but also nickel source, and CDs favors the nanocubes growth of PBAs precursor. Moreover, severe aggregation of the nanocubes and structural collapse during calcination and phosphorization have been restrained by the presence of CDs. Beneficial to the excellent electrode/electrolyte interaction and interface protection provided by the uniform CDs coating on the phosphides surfaces, rigid and porous frame of NF, synergistic effect of Ni and Fe, the optimum CDs/NiFeP-NF electrode material presents high areal specific capacitance, outstanding rate performance, and superior cycling stability. The assembled hybrid supercapacitor achieved a high energy density and power density (0.21 mWh cm−2 at 4.25 mW cm−2).

Potassium hexacyanoferrate II (K4[Fe(CN)6]), trisodium citrate dihydrate (C6H5Na3O7·2 H2O), sodium hypophosphite monohydrate（NaH2PO2·H2O）, citric acid, urea and other chemicals were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. All the reagents were of analytical grade and used as received without further purification. Nickel foam (purity of 99.8%) and activated carbon (AC) were purchased from Xinxiang Heluelida Power Materials Co., Ltd.

The CDs/NiFe PBAs nanocubes-NF was synthesized according to the procedure reported in previous literature [22]. 0.8 mmol of Potassium hexacyanoferrate II, 0.5 mmol of trisodium citrate dihydrate and 10 mg of CDs were dissolved in 20 mL of 0.2 M hydrochloric acid solution to form a homogeneous solution. Then, six pieces of NF (6 ×1 cm × 1 cm) pre-cleaned according to the reported process were immersed into the above solution and reacted at 30 ℃ for 24 h. After that, the obtained CDs/NiFe PBAs nanocubes-NF was washed with deionized water and then ethanol for several times and dried at 60 ℃ for 12 h, which was marked as CPBA. The NiFe PBAs-NF was prepared with the same routine but no addition of CDs, called as PBA.

The as-prepared CPBA and NaH2PO2·H2O were loaded at separate sides in a tube furnace, in which NaH2PO2·H2O was at the upstream position, and then heated up to different temperatures (250, 300, 350, 400 and 500 ℃) with a heating rate of 4 °C min−1 and kept at the temperature for 2 h under Ar atmosphere. Cooling down to room temperature, the CDs/NiFeP-NF were produced and labeled as CP-T, where T represents the phosphating temperature. For investigation of the roles of CDs during phosphorization, NiFeP-NF were also prepared in the similar procedure except for using the synthesized PBA as precursor, named as P-T.

The morphology, composition and nanostructure of the products were investigated with scanning electron microscopy (SEM, TM-1000) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM2100F). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy were measured by using KBr pellets on a Nicolet-Nexus 470 FTIR spectrophotometer. The crystalline phases of the sample powders scraped off from the NF were detected by X-ray diffraction (XRD, X′PertPRO diffractometer) with Ka radiation (k = 1.5418 Å). The surface chemical states of the samples were detected by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo ESCALAB 250XI).

The electrochemical performances of the as-prepared electrodes were tested in a 3 M KOH electrolyte with a three-electrode configuration by CHI 660 electrochemical workstation (Chenhua, Shanghai, China) and Land test system (LANHE, Wuhan, China). The as-synthesized samples were directly used as the working electrodes, while the saturated calomel electrode and Pt plate (1 cm × 1 cm) were adopted as the reference electrode and the counter electrode, respectively. The tests included cyclic voltammetry (CV), galvanostatic charge discharge (GCD) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS).

The areal specific capacitance of electrode material, Cs (mF cm−2), was calculated from the galvanostatic discharge curves according to the Eq. (1).
(1)
C
s =（I×t）/ΔV
where I, t andΔV represent the current density (mA cm−2), the discharge time (s) and the electrode material voltage window (V), respectively.

The hybrid supercapacitor was assembled with CDs/NiFeP-NF as the positive electrode and activated carbon (AC) as the negative electrode with 3 M KOH electrolyte. AC negative electrode was prepared via the method reported in literature [28]. The mass ratio of negative and positive electrode was about 1.4 in order to balance the charge of positive and negative electrodes. The energy density (E, mWh cm−2) and power density (P, mW cm−2) were determined based on the following Eqs:
(2)
E = 0.5×C
s ×V 2/3600
(3)
P = E×3600/Δt
whereΔt (s) is the discharge time, V (V) is the voltage.

The CDs/NiFeP-NF was fabricated in two steps as illustrated in Fig. 1. During the first co-precipitation process, Ni2+ ions supplied by NF coordinated with [Fe(CN)6]4- to form NiFe PBAs crystal nucleuses, which immediately surrounded by citric acid anion leading to negative charged surfaces. Simultaneously, the protonated CDs with positive charge combined with PBAs crystal nucleuses through electrostatic adsorption. The presence of CDs promotes the grain growth and cubic orientation, which was testified by the change of morphology between CPBA and PBA shown in Fig. S1. In subsequent calcination, the phosphorization of NiFe PBAs precursor is accomplished by PH3 released from NaH2PO2·H2O to produce the CDs/NiFeP-NF composite.

The compositions and morphologies of the as-synthesized series of samples with different calcination temperatures were investigated by XRD, FTIR and SEM. Fig. 2 shows XRD patterns of PBA, CPBA, CP-250, CP-300, P-350, CP-350, CP-400 and CP-500. Both PBA and CPBA show diffraction peaks at 17.5°, 24.8°, 35.3°, 39.8°, and 43.8°, consistent with (2 0 0), (2 2 0), (4 0 0), (4 2 0), and (4 2 2) crystal planes of NiFe PBAs (JCPDS card No. 014–0291), respectively. These characteristic diffraction peaks still exist at 300 ℃ but disappear gradually when further increasing the calcination temperature. For CP-350, CP-400 and CP-500, three weak peaks are found at 40.6°, 44.3°and 47.3°, which assigned to the (1 1 1), (2 0 1) and (2 1 0) planes of Fe2P (JCPDS card No.85–1727) and Ni2P (JCPDS card No. 75–1385), suggesting the transformation of PBA to phosphides. Additionally, the broad peak centered at 25° may be caused by the amorphous carbon generated by thermal decomposition of the cyanide ions [29]. Compared with CP-350, P-350 displays sharper diffraction peaks suggesting a higher phosphorization degree. This is ascribed to the fact that without the protection of CDs the cyanides in PBAs are more easily decomposed at same calcination temperature.

Fig. 3 shows the SEM images of CP-250, CP-300, CP-350 and P-300. For P-300, a dense coating consisted of melted nanoparticles is seen on NF surface, the porous structure of PBA precursor (Fig. S1a) is totally damaged during the calcination and phosphorization. While, CP-300 still maintain the regular nanocubes with the aid of CDs. With the increase of calcination temperature, the particle sizes of CP-T samples increase. CP-350 is composed of interconnected particles with diameter of around 300–400 nm and abundant porosity. This is also can be testified by TEM images of CP-350 ( Fig. 4b-d). Besides, the CDs are observed to disperse evenly on the surface of NiFeP. CDs and NiFeP can be identified from HRTEM images in Fig. 4e. The lattice fringes of 0.22 and 0.33 nm correspond to (111) and (200) plane of Fe2P and Ni2P, while the lattice fringe of 0.27 nm is related to CDs [20]. The EDS mapping images show that the elements of P, Fe, Ni, C, and N are homogeneously distributed in the composites (Fig. 4a).

The survey XPS spectrum of CP-350 ( Fig. 5a) further demonstrates the existence of Ni, Fe, P, C and N elements. In Ni 2p spectrum (Fig. 5b), the doublets appeared at around 857.0 eV (Ni 2p3/2) and 875.1 eV (Ni 2p1/2) are assigned to Ni2+, while the peaks at 853.2 eV (Ni 2p3/2) and 870.7 eV (Ni 2p1/2) are related to Niδ+(0 <δ < 2) of Ni-P, accompanied with two shake-up satellite (Sat.) peaks (880.5 and 862.1 eV) [5,30]. Fig. 5c shows the deconvoluted Fe 2p peaks, the peak at a binding energy of 705.5 eV is assigned to the Fe-P bonds from Fe2P [31,32]. The XPS spectrum of P 2p (Fig. 5d) displays the peaks at 129.8 eV and 134.3 eV which are ascribed to P-O and Ni/Fe-P species [33,34]. In term of the C 1 s spectrum (Fig. 5e), three characteristic peaks situated at 284.8, 286.4 and 288.7 eV, which correspond to the C-C, C-N and O-CO, respectively [35]. The N 1 s spectrum (Fig. 5f) shows three peaks of pyridinic-N (398.0 eV), pyrrolic-N (400.1 eV) and graphitic-N (401.8 eV) [14].

The FTIR spectra of PBA, CPBA, CP-250, CP-300, CP350, CP-400 and CP-500 are shown in Fig. 6. Compared with PBA, introduction of CDs enhances the absorption bands located at 1412 cm−1 in CPBA due to O-H bending mode of the oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of CDs [22]. This peak remains in all CP-T products, suggesting the presence of CDs after calcination and phosphorization. For the phosphating products of CP-T, the peak at 594 cm−1 attributed to the in-plane deformation of Fe-C disappears above 300 °C [36]. The peaks at 2097 and 2166 cm−1 attributed to the characteristic stretching vibration of the -CN group vanish at 400 °C [37], The presence of the two faint peaks in CP-350 implies that there may be traces of PBA in the phosphides. These results indicate that phosphorization of PBAs precursor is completed above 350 ℃, which are good in line with that of XRD.

The capacitive performances of the as-prepared CDs/NiFeP-NF electrodes with different calcination and phosphorization temperature were further studied. The specific capacitances are initially increased then decreased with the increase of temperature and the best is obtained for CP-350 from the galvanostatic discharge time or CV integral areas in Fig. S2a-b. At higher temperature, the phosphating products of CP-400 and CP-500 exhibit larger particle size and solid structure (Fig. S3), which would decrease the exposure of active sites and thus be harmful to the charge transfer. In order to further explore the superiority of the CDs/NiFeP-NF electrode, the capacitive properties of PBA, CPBA and P-350 and CP-350 electrode were evaluated for comparison. Fig. 7a exhibits their CV curves at a scan rate of 5 mV s−1. The redox peaks for the phosphides (P-350 and CP-350) correspond to the Faradaic reactions of both Ni2+/Ni3+ and Fe2+/Fe3+ couples [38]. In comparison of CP-350 to CPBA, the enlarged CV integral area suggests the greatly improved specific capacitance, which is originated from the contribution by additionally activated Ni2+/Ni3+ redox. Fig. 7b shows the relationship between the logarithm of anodic peak current and scan rate obtained from CV technique. The slope values (b) of the lines for PBA, CPBA, P-350 and CP-350 electrodes are calculated to be 0.49, 0.48, 0.60 and 0.63, respectively, indicating that the electrode reaction is dominated by diffusion-controlled process and the four electrodes are all battery-type electrode materials. Fig. 7c shows the galvanostatic discharge curves at a current density of 2 mA cm−2. The voltage platform appeared is also indicative of battery-type electrode materials. The higher voltage platform of phosphides (CP-350 and P-350) than that of PBA and CPBA implies more positive potential of the electrode, conductive to broaden voltage window of the supercapacitor. CP-350 has the longest discharge time among the four electrodes. Fig. 7d shows the areal specific capacitances calculated from GCD curves at different current densities. Among the four types of electrodes, CP-350 exhibit the best capacitive performances of the capacitance. The areal specific capacitances of 2589, 1920, 1629, 1189 and 1086 mF cm−2 can be obtained at current density of 2, 4, 6, 10, 16 and 20 mA cm−2, respectively. The specific capacitances of P-350 electrode (e.g. 880 at 2 mA cm−2) are much inferior to those of CP-350, even lower than that of PBA electrode (e.g. 886 at 2 mA cm−2), confirming the positive effect of CDs on the capacitance property. It is known from the above comparison of morphologies of CP-T and P-T that CDs can effectively inhibit the structure destruction during the calcination and phosphorization. Additionally, CDs can also improve the conductivity and facilitate electrolytes/electrodes interaction. As shown in Nyquist plots in Fig. 7e, CP-350 has the lowest solution resistance (R
s , 0.72) compared with PBA (0.75), CPBA (0.74) and P-350 (0.92). The larger slope of CP-350 in the low-frequency region than that of PBA, CPBA and P-350, indicates a faster ion diffusion. The cycling stabilities of PBA, CPBA, P-350 and CP-350 electrodes were evaluated via charge/discharge at the current density of 20 mA cm−2. As shown in Fig. 7f, the capacitance retention of CP-350 electrode (88%) is superior to that of P-350 (60%), PBA (50%) and CPBA (65%) after 3000 cycles. Note that the specific capacitance of CP-350 increases by 35% from the initial capacitance after 200 cycles. Even after 2100 cycle test, the final capacitance still holds the same as the initial value. Compared with the precursors (PBA and CPBA), CP-350 and P-350 have increased particle sizes and more compact structure (Figs. 3 and S1), and thus active materials have not been fully used at the initial stage [39]. By repetitive charge/discharge cycling tests, the electrochemical active Ni and Fe sites inside electrode will be gradually exposed to the electrolyte and therefore leading to the initial increase of capacitance. The capacitance of P-350 declines rapidly after 100 cycles due to the expansion and degradation of electrochemical metal phosphides. In comparison, CP-350 exhibits higher specific capacitances and more stable electrochemical performance than P-350 over the entire cycles. This may be the reason that CDs can improve the conductivity and connection between the active material and electrolyte and make full use of electrochemically active species. Moreover, CDs can also provide the protection shell to alleviate volume expansion of phosphides and structural collapse during charge and discharge processes. As shown in SEM of CP-350 after 3000 cycles, its original structure maintains perfectly (Fig. S4).

A hybrid supercapacitor was assembled based on CP-350 as positive electrode, AC as negative electrode and 3 M KOH as the electrolyte ( Fig. 8a). The different potential range of AC (−1 ∼ 0 V) and of CP-350 (0 ∼ 0.5 V) can broaden the working voltage thus improving the energy density of supercapacitor (Fig. 8b). The intensive anodic peak appears above 1.8 V due to the electrolyte splitting, and thus 0–1.7 V is applied to subsequent tests (Fig. 8c). Fig. 8d shows CV curves of the device at different scan rates in the voltage range of 0–1.7 V. As the scan rate increases, the shape of the CV curve keep well, indicating that the device presents excellent rate capability. Calculated from the GCD curves at different current densities (Fig. 8e), the areal specific capacitances are 532, 382, 327, 279, 235 and 206 mF cm−2 at 5, 10, 15, 25, 40 and 50 mA cm−2, respectively (Fig. 8f). The Ragone plot of the energy density and the power density was shown in Fig. 8g. The device delivers an energy density of 0.21 mWh cm−2 at a power density of 4.25 mW cm−2, and still retains an energy density of 0.11 mWh cm−2 at a high-power density of 21.2 mW cm−2, which are larger than or comparable to other previous literature-reported works, such as Fe-NiCoP/NF-12.5%//AC (0.33 mWh cm−2 at 1.10 mW cm−2) [40]，NiCo@Ni2Co1-LDH//AC (0.20 mWh cm−2 at 3.90 mW cm−2) [41]，Ni3S2 @Ni/CC//AC/CC (0.27 mWh cm−2 at 4.90 mW cm−2) [42]，Ni(OH)2 @CuO@Cu//reduced graphene oxide/NF (0.13 mWh cm−2 at 1.60 mW cm−2) [43]，Ni3S2//AC (0.21 mWh cm−2 at 4.44 mW cm−2) [44]，CuCo2O4/NiO//AC（0.043 mWh cm−2 at 0.40 mW cm−2）[45]，NiSe/CoSe/Ni3Se2// AC (0.33 mWh cm−2 at 7.0 mW cm−2) [46]，Co3S4 @Ni3S4//porous carbon (0.19 mWh cm−2 at 1.72 mW cm−2) [47]，CoNi2S4/Ni3S2//AC (0.29 mWh cm−2 at 3.88 mW cm−2) [48]，NiO/Ni(OH)2/polymer//ordered mesoporous carbon/carbon fiber (0.011 mWh cm−2 at 0.33 mW cm−2) [49] and Ti3C2-Cu//Ti3C2-Cu (0.103 mWh cm−2 at 0.8 mW cm−2) [50]. Fig. 8h displays the electrochemical stability of the device. The capacitance of the device increases in the first 700 cycles and the retention remains approximately 88% after 3000 charge/discharge cycles. It can be clearly seen that the capacitance retention went up to 125% after 700 laps of the long cycle, mainly because the positive electrode is gradually activated at the initial stage of charge/discharge [51]. An electrical motor fan can be powered and LED lights be illuminated by this device, showing the practical application of the supercapacitors (the inset in Fig. 8h).

In summary, a binder-free CDs/NiFeP-NF electrode was fabricated by a simple phosphating treatment of a precursor of CDs/NiFe PBA nanocubes-NF for the application of hybrid supercapacitors. The presence of CDs is found to effectively inhibit the growth of large particles and solid structures during phosphating process. At 350 ℃, the optimum product of CP-350 exhibits outstanding areal specific capacitance of 2589 mF cm−2 at 2 mA cm−2, superior rate performance and significantly enhanced stability due to the porous structure, synergistic effect of Ni and Fe elements [37], and enhanced electrolyte/electrode interaction and interface protection resulting from the unique properties of CDs [52]. Based on this novel electrode, the as-assembled hybrid supercapacitor presents wide potential window, remarkable energy density and power density. The structure modulation in this work will bring a hopeful way to push the development of PBA-based phosphides materials for energy conversion and storage.

Pengyun Jia: Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Hongchang Yao: Funding acquisition. Qingchao Liu: Resources. Jianshe Wang: Validation. Zhongjun Li: Project administration, Funding acquisition. Lin Zhang: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported in part by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21974125).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jallcom.2023.170525.

ce:section.ce:para:

.

EnergyChem
